speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have still to sort out the pictures. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: They're here in the envelope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. The same envelope I saw the other day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you the-- the-- the criminals and the victims, all in one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like-- [AUDIO OUT] --25, 1997. The interviewee, Mrs. Zelda Hait, nee Shebshelovitz. The interviewer, Nina Elazar-Wolff. The interview is taking place in Bat-Yam, Israel in the English language. February 25, 1997. The interviewee, Mrs. Hait Zelda, nee Shebshelovitz. The interviewer, Nina Elazar-Wolff. The interview takes place in Bat-Yam, Israel in the English language. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce yourself by name, last name, where were you born, date, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm Zelda Hait, nee Shebselovitz, born on the 19th of August, 1920 in Latvia-- Kuldiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who are your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Yosef and Nehama Shebshelovitz, a very Jewish family. Ardent Zionists who brought us up in a Zionist spirit. We were three children. My brother, who eventually became [NON-ENGLISH] Betar. My little sister, was later murdered by the Germans. And had also [NON-ENGLISH] Betar. And I was a student when the Germans arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us what was your mother's name from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Josephson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your parents born, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: All my family members were born in Kuldiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us the names of your brother and sister, please? And when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was born in-- Zelig was born in 1915, after the First World War had started. And was exiled-- I wanted to say he was exiled as a Jewish baby with his parents to Belarussia, accused-- like all people in Kuldiga-- of espionage for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which year was it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1915. It was the first war. They were exiled for being German friends. In the second war, they were killed for being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did they come back home from the exile?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came back home-- they experienced the Russian revolution in 1917 over there. And when Latvia became independent again in 1918, they returned to the homeland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the girls were born afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in 1920 and my little sister was born in '22. Her name was Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us, please, what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food-- delicatessen food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember anything from his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. We had two shops. And I used to help my parents. We owned a house of your own-- of our own. We had astro by the plantation and we kids were used to hard work. So we took care of the-- of the strawberry plantation, of the garden, of the business. And helped him make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your family-- what kind of family were you? In the sense of the atmosphere. Were you were well off? What-- could you describe us a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a middle class family who worked hard for a living. And we lived like that-- quite wealthy-- until the year of 1931, '32, when the Great Depression beg-- started. Then we lost all our property and we were out in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning that we had to get a place to live in. We-- I started to work at the age of 11, giving lessons-- Latvian lessons to little children. And my brother also started working, although he was a student. And life became very tough to all the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what respect was your family in-- in comparison to the Jewish community in Kuldiga? How were they placed or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were placed like honorable Jewish people. Trad-- we had a traditional house. My father used to go twice a year on Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, to synagogue, and so did my mother. And I was the one-- I was the one who knew already-- could read Hebrew. So I was in a synagogue and was reading the Seder and all the Jewish ladies who didn't speak Hebrew were repeating what I was-- my prayers. That was my duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us, please, what was the-- how big was the family as a whole? I mean, brothers, sisters, aunties.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So my brother-- my father had four brothers. One of them immigrated to America at the beginning of the century because he was supposed to become a Russian soldier for 25 years and Jewish youngsters fled Russia. He had another brother, Shai, who also lived in Kuldiga, with a family of two daughters and a son. Then was a-- a brother-- a childless brother, Moshe. And an uncle in Riga-- Herman-- the most educated one, who had a son Robert. And both of them were killed the first night the Germans occupied Riga. All of the mentioned people were killed or crippled before they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's family were lucky to be over in Scotland. They-- they went to Scotland at the turn of the century. So all the children were born there. And-- and she had there a sister and two brothers. So they were-- no war victims. And we were in touch. We were in very close touch-- a very loving-- loving family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit, if you remember, about the Kuldiga Jewish community? How many Jews, what kind of style life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population in Kuldiga was very similar to the German population in Kuldiga because there were fewer Latvians-- natives-- than Jews and Germans. Actually, it was regarded as a German town. For instance, my mother's friend-- lady friends-- were all German. German was spoken in the house. We recited German poems and there was no anti-Semitism on behalf of those Germans living in Kuldiga. Very friendly, I would say. It was a typical provincial life, although it didn't remind me of [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland. It was absolutely different. People were very cultured, well-bred, educated, I would say. And led a nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Kuldiga at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe about 300 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was the population of Kuldiga?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 8,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you spoke German at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a home, from the religious point of view-- Jewish point of view-- was your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Candles were lit to Erev Shabbat. So as I mentioned before, my mother and father went to synagogue, and so did we, the children. I attended, as well as my sister and brother, a Jewish primary school. The language-- subjects were taught in German, but we had a Hebrew rabbi who came three times a week and we learned [NON-ENGLISH] and we could make ourselves understood in Old Hebrew. Mind you, that's a big difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Mr. Sekunda, Herr Sekunda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he died here in this country. Yes. S-E-K-U-N-D-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. That's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: A former halutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. Can you remember your brother's bar mitzvah, for instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can describe it. Unfortunately, it was a very sad bar mitzvah because my mother gave birth, the same day, to a little boy, who was born-- was premature born. And this was the reason why the bar mitzvah was celebrated only in synagogue, but not at home. And I remember exactly the day. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it? How was it done in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was-- he was asked to come to the [INAUDIBLE] and do the prayers. And my father was [NON-ENGLISH], as I said. And according to Jewish rules and laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go back to the anti-Semitic point of view, did you feel any anti-Semitism while being a child brought up in Kuldiga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did, but only on behalf of the Latvians, not on behalf of the Germans. Until 1933, all-- we'll say well-to-do or less well-to-do Jewish people sent their children to a German high school, which was private, led by the director [? Bukovnyov. ?] And my brother graduated from that school and spoke a very beautiful German. In 1933, there were already attacks against Jewish youngsters. The [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH]. And immediately, Jewish people started-- stopped sending their children to that school. So when my turn came in 1933, I was sent-- like all other Jewish children-- to a Latvian high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No special anti-Semitism. But the best compliment you could hear was oh, you are not like a bloody Jewish. You are like one of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or if Jewish boys were making noise in the classroom-- so the German teacher, I remember, said-- she didn't say, David Abramovich, shut up or what. She said, Jews, will you shut your mouths? So in a-- to such an extent, it was very unpleasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, the Jewish children were excluded from school because they couldn't catch up with the Latvian language. And unfortunately, I was the only girl who remained. And there was another boy who remained. He's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this school a mixed school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what do you mean by-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] boys and girls, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boys and girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us the names of the schools you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schools?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. The school-- the primary school and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [INTERPOSING VOICES] [NON-ENGLISH] It means the Jewish primary school of Kuldiga. And [NON-ENGLISH]. That is the state gymnasium of Latvia. It was considered one of the best schools in the whole country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else lived with you in the house on a permanent basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an apartment. Is that what you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My beloved grandma-- my father's mother-- she died as a very old lady and she was blind. And we shared every experience-- every sweet, every chocolate with her. And we had to take her around the house because she was absolutely blind. We loved her so much. She died in 1938, luckily. So I closed her eyes when she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Describe her? Yes. Her name was Ruchel Leah and she was called Leah. She was very wise and people came to her to ask her what her estimate is. How old someone's neighbor could be, for instance. Because she-- because-- due to her age, she always gave the right idea-- people-- what to do. People came to ask her for a piece of advice because she was so wise. And she was very loving. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nurse-- nannies at home with you or servants at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When my parents were well-off-- we had two servants in the house-- Ieva and her mother. The two of them were working for us. And Ieva was actually the nanny, too. And she took good care of the children. But she loved-- since she was not married-- a single person-- she loved me as her own child. And people in Kuldiga used to gossip that I am not a Jewish child, but a goyish child. That I am her child. Because she took me to church on Sunday and all the services, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell her name for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With pleasure. I-E-V-A, Ieva. Sesietinsh is her surname-- capital S-E-S-I-E-T-I-N-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And you mentioned that you lived in your mother tongue was German at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am afraid that I have two mother tongues, as an exception. If asked what my mother tongue is, I really don't know where I'm telling the truth and where I am lying. Because the first language I spoke was Latvian. Until the age of six, I spoke Latvian. And then while I was supposed to go to school the following day, my mother-- who spoke a rotten Latvian-- came up to me and said, my child, it is a disgrace. You are a Jewish child and you can't speak German. So I went to school and the next day, I spoke fluent-- German fluently. I knew it but I did not speak it. So I have two mother tongues, also happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My-- my parents' friends were Yiddish speaking people, as well as German speaking people. And I learned to write in Yiddish by myself because I knew the Hebrew alphabet. And I used to write letters for the poor people of Kuldiga to their American relatives for help. And I-- with great pleasure, wherever I could, I spoke Yiddish until the day I left Riga for Israel. I loved the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of a youth movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I was a member of Betar as a very young child, wearing a uniform, like my sister and my brother. And we sang and danced all the songs of the Betarim and we knew exactly what was going on in Palestine. And we did the Horah and we celebrated everything that was connected with Judaism and Palestine. We knew everything about [INAUDIBLE] and what happened in this country. I even remember a song in Yiddish, which no-- nobody in this country knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recite it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please do. [YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And now [INAUDIBLE] in prison. [YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't sing because I sing as a cow. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Till what age did you stay in Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very-- I left it very early. Until the age of 13, 14. When I started thinking and understanding things, it was not enough-- it was a bit a limited approach. I was a Zionist and I remained a Zionist until this day. But I didn't quite agree with the ideas of the Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you grew up in a Zionistic home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sense was it Zionistic?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. My father was an ardent Revisionist. And all of the gatherings-- when Jabotinsky came to Latvia, on two occasions, my father and my brother made a long trip to Liepaja, which is also in [NON-ENGLISH] in order to hear Vladimir-- he was called. Vladimir Ze'ev Jabotinksy. And my brother has not forgotten his speech. One speech he made, in-- by the way-- in German. Yes. So my father participated-- well, in all events that are connected with Palestine, Judaism, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it when they went to Liepaja?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was 1936, but I'm not quite sure. In mid-- in the mid '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you finish school in-- in-- Kuldiga?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had decided to become a teacher of foreign languages when I was a child. And I was penniless-- absolutely penniless because, occasionally, I had to leave school and to go and work in a factory to earn some money to pay the tuition fee. I didn't know how I would manage, but I went to Riga, I became a stu-- and Jewish students were not admitted easily to the college. So you had to pass an examination, which I did. I-- I lived in a small room of four square meters. I gave English lessons and I did needlework for people. I knitted gloves. And so I earned my livelihood. And each six weeks, I went to Kuldiga to see my family. So-- so far about it. {BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you studying in Riga. When did you start studying there?

speaker: interviewer
text: September.

speaker: interviewee
text: September, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the school or college?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a college. English College of Riga. Our professors were all Englishmen. Only-- later on, one was a [NON-ENGLISH] And I still remember the names of those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you manage to finish the college?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was in my-- in my last year when the war broke out. And I finished it when I returned to Latvia after the war. I passed all examinations apart from-- apart from the history of the Communist party. And-- because I was arrested. So I remained without a diploma. But in the Latvian newspaper, there was a shark-- [LAUGHS] --a short article that Zelma [NON-ENGLISH]-- Zelma [NON-ENGLISH] passed her examinations brilliantly. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's nice. Can you describe a little bit the style life in Riga while you were a student from '38 onwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. But from what point of view?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your social life-- how did you went into society-- student society, Jewish society, Jewish student society.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I associated only with Jewish students because I couldn't find any common language with Latvians. I knew they were anti-Semitic and there was nothing to talk with them about. The young ladies who were students at the college were from very rich Jewish homes and I was not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't, often, meet them. Because while they were throwing parties, enjoying themselves, I was sitting in my four room-- four square meter room and working very hard. So I had a couple of friends-- boys and the girls. So we met in-- not in cafe-- neither in cafes nor restaurant, as today. Nor in discotheques. At home, just discussing things. Having read books and discussing books. It was all very serious-- lovely, but serious. And what was the atmosphere in Riga at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the late '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In '38 onwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We knew already what was going on in Germany. I was, obviously, too young to realize it-- to realize that it could happen to us. And my father, who used to listen to the BBC very many times a day, Lindley Fraser was a commentator in the German language. And he always quoted him. But it didn't occur to my father that this could happen to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the war broke out, we were taken by surprise that the Germans are coming to occupy the country. And yet, my father said, we have already been exiled to Russia. And we saw, during the revolution, what is going on in Russia-- all the bloodshed. What are the Germans going to do to us-- the German speaking people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the worst case, they would make us work-- hard labor. This was the thought of all German speaking Jews. And this was a tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Germany occupied Poland-- September '39 onwards-- what was the atmosphere in Riga? How did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't-- I remember the day. [NON-ENGLISH]. I was just giving a Latvian lesson. I was watching what was going on. It was in Kudliga because I was still on holiday. Nothing has changed, as if war-- a war had not broken out. When I came to Riga to go on studying, nothing absolutely. Only when the Russians occupied already Riga, then we felt it's because all our English professors said, we are going to leave this God damned country. They were offered-- suggested they stay there. And Mr. Turner-- or one of our professors said, in this country, not for any prize. But there was war, as they couldn't get back to England. So they went to Africa-- over Africa, over Asia someplace, in order to get back to-- later on-- to-- to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with whom did you study then? If all those--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were-- there were some Jewish ladies who had studied English abroad and who were competent enough to teach us. There was an old English lady-- non-Jewish lady-- Mrs. Lindor, with whom I was on very friendly terms. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She-- she was-- also became one of the teachers. I would say that in 1940, the level was absolutely different. Although these people were-- are very educated, their expertise was not the same as that of the professors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from the Jewish point of view lifestyle, did you have any Jewish lifestyle, like Chagim and going to the synagogue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and Shabbat and-- during all this time in Riga?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not in Riga. Only when I came to Kuldiga. Because in-- in Riga, I-- I only knew where the synagogue is because all Chagim I spent in Kuldiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. And while you were in Riga, till the broke war-- the war broke out in this part of the world, what happened to your parents, to your brother, sister? Where were they? How did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the out-- outbreak-- the-- actually the outbreak of the occupation? My brother and my-- my sister-- and my sister had just finished high school, with the gold medal that I've told you. And she was about to come to Riga to study at the university. But since she was taken by surprise during-- by the war, they stayed on all together. My brother decided to send the family to Russia, somewhere in the rear. The evacuation. But my parents could not go any place without me. They decided to take me with them. So they make-- made a stop in Riga. And we saw it is impossible to get to the train-- to get on the train. Because the Russian officers wanted to evacuate their wives and all the crowd they had. And we had-- there was no way out. We had-- we were stuck in Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother-- at the same time, where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was the one who first seated the family on the truck and made sure they leave. And he was sure they are somewhere in Russia. And only then did he get on the last truck and went-- went to Russia with some other people who escaped the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, the pam-- the family was separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was when, with the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the 27th of-- of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: --41. So here we come to the stage of you being in Riga and the parents and your sister in Kuliga.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they come--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they come to-- they come to join me.

speaker: interviewer
text: To join you. When did they come to join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days, I believe-- two days or three days prior to the 27th. So it could be 24th or 25th of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, at the end of June of '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. War broke out in 22-- on the 22nd. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any people who really influenced you at that stage of your life-- I mean, before the war broke out-- any Jews or non-Jews in Riga who really were influential in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what sense, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Socially, romantically. Any sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had a boyfriend who shared my opinion, who was also Jew-- also Jewish and also a Zionist. And all the girls I met, actually, were all-- shared my opinion, or I shared theirs. There were no differences. We didn't have any people who didn't want to know anything about Palestine, about Judaism. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: We come to the end of the cassette number one. And we'll take over immediately cassette number two. Thank you.
